1: Psalm 66 pivots midway from communal hymn (vv1-12) to personal testimony (vv13-20) — the universal "all the earth" to the individual "I." The opening: הָרִיעוּ לֵאלֹהִים כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (hārîʿû lêʾlōhîm kol-hāʾāreṣ, "Shout joyfully to God, all the earth!") — rûaʿ (hiphil: to raise the acclamation-shout — the battle-cry of celebration, the coronation shout): all the earth called to raise the universal shout of divine kingship. The scope: not Israel only but all the earth (kol-hāʾāreṣ).
2: זַמְּרוּ כְבוֹד-שְׁמוֹ (zammĕrû kĕbôd-shĕmô, "Sing the glory of his name") — zāmar: to sing with instruments; kĕbôd-shĕmô: "the glory of his name" — the reputation and weight (kābôd) of the divine name proclaimed in song. שִׂימוּ כָבוֹד תְּהִלָּתוֹ (śîmû kābôd tĕhillātô, "make his praise resplendent") — śîm kābôd: "put weight/glory on" — making the praise itself glorious. The language of weighting the praise with the full dignity of what God has done.
3: אִמְרוּ לֵאלֹהִים מַה-נּוֹרָא מַעֲשֶׂיךָ (ʾimrû lêʾlōhîm māh-nôrāʾ maʿăśêkā, "Say to God, 'How awesome are your deeds!'") — nôrāʾ: awesome, fear-inducing (the holy fear produced by encountering divine power — as in Psalms 65:5). בְּרֹב עֻזְּךָ יְכַחֲשׁוּ-לְךָ אֹיְבֶיךָ (bĕrōb ʿuzzĕkā yĕkaḥăshû-lĕkā ʾōyĕbêkā, "through the greatness of your power your enemies cower before you") — kāḥash (piel: to cringe, to deny, to submit falsely): the enemies making a reluctant submission — not willing worship but the forced cringing of the defeated before irresistible power. Even reluctant submission is part of the divine sovereignty.
4: כָּל-הָאָרֶץ יִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לְךָ (kol-hāʾāreṣ yishtaḥăwû lĕkā, "All the earth bows down before you") — shāḥāh (hishtaphel: to bow down, to prostrate in worship): the universal prostration of all the earth. יְזַמְּרוּ-לָךְ יְזַמְּרוּ שְׁמֶךָ (yĕzammĕrû-lāk yĕzammĕrû shĕmekā, "they sing to you, they sing praises to your name") — the double zāmar: the first directed to "you" (God himself), the second to "your name" (the articulated divine character and reputation). Selāh.
5: לְכוּ וּרְאוּ מִפְעֲלוֹת אֱלֹהִים (lĕkû ûrĕʾû mipʿălōt ʾĕlōhîm, "Come and see what God has done") — the invitation to witness (as in Psalms 46:8: "Come, behold the works of the LORD"). Pōʿal: works, deeds. נוֹרָא עֲלִילָה עַל-בְּנֵי אָדָם (nôrāʾ ʿălîlāh ʿal-bĕnê ʾādām, "he is awesome in his acts toward humanity") — ʿălîlāh: a deed, an act (often a great/mighty act — Isaiah 12:4; Psalms 9:11). The divine deeds done "upon" (ʿal) humanity — performed for human benefit and human witness.
6: הָפַךְ יָם לְיַבָּשָׁה (hāpak yām lĕyabbāshāh, "He turned the sea into dry land") — hāpak: to turn, to overturn (the transforming reversal): the Exodus event (Exodus 14:21-22 — the sea turned to dry land for the crossing). בַּנָּהָר יַעַבְרוּ בְרָגֶל (bannāhār yaʿabrû bĕrāgel, "they crossed the river on foot") — possibly the Jordan crossing under Joshua (Joshua 3:14-17) — two great water-crossings on foot cited as definitive acts of divine power. שָׁם נִשְׂמְחָה-בּוֹ (shām niśmĕḥāh-bô, "there we rejoiced in him") — the place of crossing is the place of joy: the sea crossed = rejoicing in God. Exodus 15:1-21 is the great rejoicing at the sea.
7: מֹשֵׁל בִּגְבוּרָתוֹ עוֹלָם (mōshêl bigĕbûrātô ʿôlām, "He rules by his power forever") — māshal: to rule, to govern (the divine governance of history). Gĕbûrāh: might, heroic strength (as in Psalms 65:6). עֵינָיו בַּגּוֹיִם תִּצְפֶּינָה (ʿênāyw baggôyim tiṣpêynāh, "his eyes watch over the nations") — ṣāpāh: to watch, to spy out, to look out from a height (the watchtower-verb: God as the elevated watchman observing all the nations). הַסּוֹרְרִים אַל-יָרוּמוּ לָמוֹ סֶלָה (hassôrĕrîm ʾal-yārûmû lāmô selāh, "let the rebellious not exalt themselves") — sôrēr: the stubborn rebel (Deuteronomy 21:18, 20 — the rebellious son). The rebellious nations under the divine eye cannot ultimately exalt themselves.
8: בָּרְכוּ עַמִּים אֱלֹהֵינוּ (bārĕkû ʿammîm ʾĕlōhênû, "Bless our God, O peoples!") — the summons addressed to ʿammîm (peoples — the plural including all nations) to bless "our God" (ʾĕlōhênû — Israel's God claimed as the universal God). וְהַשְׁמִיעוּ קוֹל תְּהִלָּתוֹ (wĕhashmiʿû qôl tĕhillātô, "let the sound of his praise be heard") — shāmaʿ (hiphil: to cause to be heard): making the praise audible — the sound of praise as the evidence of God's reality.
9: הַשָּׂם נַפְשֵׁנוּ בַּחַיִּים (hashśām napshênû baḥayyîm, "He keeps our lives in life") — śûm (hiphil participle: the one who places/sets): God as the one who places the nāpesh (life) within life itself — sustaining existence from within. וְלֹא-נָתַן לַמּוֹט רַגְלֵנוּ (wĕlōʾ-nātan lammôṭ raglênû, "and does not allow our feet to stumble") — māṭ: to totter (as in Psalms 62:2 — the firm foundation that prevents the great shaking). God not allowing the foot to slip: the preserving providence that holds the community upright.
10: כִּי-בְחַנְתָּנוּ אֱלֹהִים (kî-bĕḥantānû ʾĕlōhîm, "For you, O God, have tested us") — bāḥan: to test, to examine (the assay of metal — the same root as bōḥan ʾeben, "testing stone" of Isaiah 28:16). צְרַפְתָּנוּ כִּצְרָף-כָּסֶף (ṣĕraptānû kiṣrāp-kesep, "you have refined us as silver is refined") — ṣārap: to refine (to smelt metal and remove impurities — the same refining image as Malachi 3:3: "he will sit as a refiner and purifier of silver"). The suffering of vv11-12 is the furnace of refining: painful but producing purer metal. The suffering is not purposeless but productive.
11: הֲבֵאתָנוּ בַמְּצוּדָה (hăbêʾtānû bammĕṣûdāh, "you brought us into the net") — mĕṣôdāh: a net, a trap (the mesh that catches and holds — the image of captivity or siege). God himself bringing his people into the net — the enemy's captivity as the divine instrument of refining. שַׂמְתָּ מוּעָקָה בְמָתְנֵינוּ (śamtā mûʿāqāh bĕmātnênû, "you laid a crushing burden on our backs") — mûʿāqāh: a pressed/crushing burden (hapax — from ʿûq: to press down). The burden that bends the back — the oppression.
12: הִרְכַּבְתָּ אֱנוֹשׁ לְרֹאשֵׁנוּ (hirkkabtā ʾĕnôsh lĕrōʾshênû, "you let men ride over our heads") — rākab (hiphil: to cause to ride): the humiliating image of conquerors riding over the prostrate defeated — complete subjugation (Isaiah 51:23: "I will put it into the hand of your tormentors who said to you, 'Bow down, that we may pass over you'"). בָּאנוּ-בָאֵשׁ וּבַמַּיִם (bāʾnû-bāʾêsh ûbammayim, "we went through fire and through water") — the two elemental trials (Isaiah 43:2: "When you pass through the waters, I will be with you; and through the rivers, they shall not overwhelm you; when you walk through fire you shall not be burned"). Both extremes: the destroying fire and the drowning water. וַתּוֹצִיאֵנוּ לָרְוָיָה (wattôṣîʾênû lārĕwāyāh, "yet you brought us out to a wide and spacious place") — rĕwāyāh: saturation, abundance, the spacious place (from rāwāh: to be saturated — as in Psalms 65:10: the drenched field). The reversal: from fire and water to the well-watered wide place of abundance. The silver refined comes out pure.
13: At v13 the psalm shifts from communal "we" to personal "I" — the individual believer's testimony: אָבוֹא בֵיתְךָ בְעוֹלוֹת (ʾābôʾ bêtĕkā bĕʿôlôt, "I will come into your house with burnt offerings") — ʿôlāh: the burnt offering (wholly consumed on the altar — the total-surrender offering). The entering the house of God with the sacrifice pledged in crisis. אֲשַׁלֵּם לְךָ נְדָרָי (ʾăshallêm lĕkā nĕdāray, "I will fulfill my vows to you") — shālam (piel: to complete, to pay): the vows paid in full (as in Psalms 61:8; Psalms 65:1). The covenant of crisis vows repaid at the sanctuary.
14: אֲשֶׁר-פָּצוּ שְׂפָתַי (ʾăsher-pāṣû śĕpātay, "the vows that my lips uttered") — pāṣāh: to open wide (the lips parting in the urgent promise of crisis). וְדִבֶּר-פִּי בַּצַּר-לִי (wĕdibber-pî baṣṣar-lî, "and my mouth spoke in my time of trouble") — ṣar: the tight-place, the distress (as in Psalms 18:6 — the narrow place of the enemy's pressing). The vow spoken in the tight-place, now to be honored in the wide-place (v12: rĕwāyāh).
15: עֹלוֹת מֵחִים אַעֲלֶה-לָּךְ (ʿôlôt mêḥîm ʾaʿăleh-llāk, "I will offer you burnt offerings of fattened animals") — mêḥîm: the fat ones (fully fattened animals — the finest of the flock). עִם-קְטֹרֶת אֵילִים (ʿim-qĕṭōret ʾêlîm, "with the rising smoke of rams") — qĕṭōret: incense, fragrant smoke (the ascending smoke of sacrifice as the visual image of prayer rising to God — Psalms 141:2; Revelation 8:4). Selāh marks the pause before the invitation to witness.
16: לְכוּ-שִׁמְעוּ וַאֲסַפְּרָה (lĕkû-shîmʿû waʾăsappĕrāh, "Come and hear, and I will tell") — the personal testimony opened to the community: sāpar: to count, to recount, to narrate (as in Psalms 22:30-31 — the future generation will declare/sāpar God's deeds). כָּל-יִרְאֵי אֱלֹהִים (kol-yirʾê ʾĕlōhîm, "all you who fear God") — the audience: the God-fearing community (yirʾê ʾĕlōhîm — the ones whose inner orientation is holy fear). אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה לְנַפְשִׁי (ʾăsher ʿāśāh lĕnapshî, "what he has done for me") — the personal testimony: the specific deeds done for the individual nāpesh.
17: אֵלָיו פִּי-קָרָאתִי (ʾêlāyw pî-qārāʾtî, "I cried out to him with my mouth") — the sequence: crisis cry → divine answer. וְרוֹמַם תַּחַת לְשׁוֹנִי (wĕrômam taḥat lĕshônî, "and his praise was on my tongue") — rômam: exaltation (the lifting-up of God in praise — the praise was already forming on the tongue while the cry was being made). The prayer and the praise simultaneous: the mouth cries and the tongue praises.
18: אָוֶן אִם-רָאִיתִי בְלִבִּי (ʾāwen ʾim-rāʾîtî bĕlibbî, "If I had cherished sin in my heart") — rāʾāh (qal: to see — here: to look at with approval, to cherish, to regard fondly): not accidentally sinning but deliberately cultivating sin in the heart. ʾĀwen: harmful iniquity (as in Psalms 58:2; Psalms 64:2 — the trouble/harm that one works). לֹא יִשְׁמַע אֲדֹנָי (lōʾ yishmaʿ ʾădōnāy, "the Lord would not have listened") — the prayer-condition: the cherished, hidden sin in the heart renders prayer unheard. John 9:31: "We know that God does not listen to sinners; but if anyone is a worshiper of God and does his will, God listens to him." The condition is not sinless perfection but the non-cherishing of known sin.
19: אָכֵן שָׁמַע אֱלֹהִים (ʾākên shāmaʿ ʾĕlōhîm, "But truly God has heard") — ʾākên: truly, surely (the strong affirmation — like ʾak of Psalms 62:1). The condition of v18 was NOT met: the Psalmist did not cherish sin, and so God heard. הִקְשִׁיב בְּקוֹל תְּפִלָּתִי (hiqshîb bĕqôl tĕpillātî, "he has attended to the voice of my prayer") — qāshab (hiphil: to incline the ear, to pay careful attention): God's attentive ear to the prayer's voice — not merely acknowledging but listening with care.
20: בָּרוּךְ אֱלֹהִים (bārûk ʾĕlōhîm, "Blessed be God!") — the closing benediction: the Psalmist blesses God in return for being heard. אֲשֶׁר לֹא-הֵסִיר תְּפִלָּתִי וְחַסְדּוֹ מֵאִתִּי (ʾăsher lōʾ-hêsîr tĕpillātî wĕḥasdô mêʾittî, "who has not turned away my prayer or withheld his steadfast love from me") — the double negative celebration: God did NOT turn away the prayer, and God did NOT withhold his ḥesed. The psalm that began with the universal shout of all the earth (v1) ends with the individual's personal testimony that the ḥesed — the covenant-loyalty — reaches even into individual crisis and is not withheld. The universal scope and the intimate personal are united in the one ḥesed.
